Chloen’s voice was small. “Is it… like what you did?”

Jhace’s hands flexed once, involuntary. The vibration tried to rise. He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.

The measurer tapped her pencil against the slate once—one controlled knock, like a signal to herself. “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”

Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.

Below, the distant city’s bells—faint, delayed by distance and air—began to ring. A thin sequence from far away layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.

Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two pulses.

Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.

His sternum was already too awake.

He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.

He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.

Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.

And now he knew it could be done.

A small metal hook clattered on stone near his foot—something from a brace timber that had fallen loose.

Jhace's hands flexed before he could stop them.

Not toward the hook because it mattered. Toward it because his body had learned what holding felt like when the pull hit—how the lock made the world go quiet for one breath—and it wanted that quiet back.

He took a half-step toward it and caught himself, jaw clenched at the betrayal.

The hook lifted a fraction off the pavement.

It was not control. It was reflex.

He forced his fingers open. The hook dropped and rang once. His hands shook harder after, not from fear but from absence.

No one saw it.

But he felt it.

And the copper taste surged again, sharp and insistent, like his body was trying to warn him about what he was becoming.

---

# Chapter 7 — Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting

The upper terrace had turned into a sorting floor.

Not formal. Not clean. Just the kind of order you made when you were trying to stop a city from bleeding itself out through a thousand small failures.

Brace teams moved in lanes. Runners carried slates. People dragged what could be dragged: water skins, pry bars, a child with a wrist wrapped in cloth so tight the fingers had gone pale.

Tiffani stood at the seam line where the terrace met the next rise and watched the crack behave.

It had been a clean fracture earlier. Now it forked. The fork was new.

She set her slate against her thigh and tried to trust her last baseline.

Ambient hum: high. Particulate: high. Crowd density: high.

Checklist says: same conditions.

So she acted like it.

*Seam crack stable. Rate: slow. No immediate drop.*

A pulse hit the terrace—pull, then slack—hard enough to make the crowd sway as one body. The crack widened a hair, then closed again like a mouth.

Her last baseline did not include *closing cracks*.

She retested.

Not because she wanted to. Because the fork in the crack was a retest trigger, and she had written that rule for a reason.

She took three reads: breath-spaced, same angle, same stance. She tagged confidence, then watched the tag rot in real time.

*Seam displacement: reversible. Confidence: low.*

Protocol drift. The world was changing faster than her checklists could keep up.

Her jaw locked once. She released it before it could become a tell.

A brace captain called for her.

"Observatory," he said—not her name, not a title, just the function they needed. "We're gathering. Now."

Tiffani closed the slate halfway before moving, keeping the face angled down. Habit. Discipline. The kind you practiced when the wrong eyes were present.

Meeting = control surface, she reminded herself.

People used meetings to assign blame the way they used nails to pin cloth to a wall. Fast. Efficient. Cruel.

She walked into the semicircle.

Pella was already there, a smudge of grit across one cheek and a broken stylus stuck behind her ear. Her eyes met Tiffani's for a beat: tired acknowledgment, not comfort.

Jhace stood two bodies back from the center, hands low, wedge bar braced against his boot. His fingers looked steady while he held the tool. When he let go, the vibration showed itself again in the empty air.

Tiffani's attention slid toward that tremor—automatic, unwanted.

Her knee flared—a hot line pulled tight behind the joint. She'd felt that flare before: the body's receipt for a thought she wasn't allowed to file. She locked the leg straight, set her weight evenly, and kept her face neutral.

Not relevant to seam crack. Not relevant to meeting control.

She pulled her gaze back to the brace captain's mouth and forced her pencil hand still against the slate edge until the micro-pull passed.

Observer bias.

Contamination.

A factor spoke first. "We need a classification," he said. "What do we call this pull and slack? How do we brief it? People are panicking."

Snap classification was a temptation. A label to give the crowd so they would stop making their own.

Tiffani could feel the pressure of eyes on her slate, even when no one was looking directly at it.

She answered anyway—fast, clipped.

"Phase cycling," she said. "Pull. Slack. Repeat."

It sounded cleaner than it was.

The factor’s rings flashed as he clenched his hand. “What happened?” he said. “Not the cycling—the first thing. Before the bells.”

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on the crack line. “Transit event. Core-penetrating. Exit-face rupture at azimuth twenty-seven.”

Silence.

She glanced up. Saw blank faces. Realized they hadn’t been watching the sky-frame when it tore open. Hadn’t measured the pressure drop before the pull.

Her pencil tapped once against the slate edge—a calibration tick she did not mean to make.

“Something passed through the mass,” she said, flat. “Not from inside. Not from above. Through. Entry face unknown. Exit face here.” Her chin tipped toward the Rift hanging black in blue. “Pressure wave preceded visual. Structural liquefaction followed. Particulate suspension ongoing. The air thinned. The pull hit. Then slack. Then pull again.”

The priest’s mouth worked. “Through… the world?”

“Through the mass,” Tiffani corrected. “Like a nail through timber. Entry. Exit. The nail is gone. The hole remains.”

She looked back down and wrote, small, for her eyes only: *audience baseline deficit*.

A runner interrupted her before the room could turn that explanation into a story.

"Stone moved wrong," the runner said. "Up. Not down. Like the load didn't know where the ground was."

A murmur rolled through the semicircle, the crowd trying to make a story out of it.

Tiffani's mind reached for the last protocol that fit: slope failure, brace slip, misread vector.

Checklist says: mass follows gravity.

So she wanted to say: witness error.

She did not.

She had learned what it cost to dismiss witnesses when the grid itself was lying.

"How many saw it?" she asked.

"Three brace teams," the runner said. "And the water barrel. It rolled uphill. Two strides."

Tiffani opened her slate wider—not for the crowd. For herself.

She wrote, small and hard:

*Event: mass-water vector deviation. Multiple witnesses. Confidence: medium. Do not assign cause.*

Then she closed the slate again.

Because the act of writing in front of the semicircle changed the semicircle. Turned fear into testimony. Turned testimony into ammunition.

She would not feed that machine if she could help it.

The brace captain looked at Jhace. "You were under it," he said. "You said something about—"

Jhace's mouth opened, then closed, as if words had failed to queue.

The factor exhaled through his nose. "That's not possible."

Jhace didn't look at him. "It happened."

Tiffani did not write *Jhace said*. She did not write *held*. She did not write *impossible*.

She wrote:

*Subject reports constraint event. Mechanism unverified.*

Constraint language protected the subject. Mechanism language made a target.

She lifted her eyes back to the semicircle and spoke in the same tone she used when telling a counter to reset a baseline.

"We have three immediate problems," she said. "Structure. Air. People."

Two fingers, not dramatic. Directional.

"Structure: mark cracks. Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped. Do not stand under façades."

"Air: when the pull comes, breathe shallow. When it slacks, fill. Teach it. Make it habit."

"People: reunite who you can. Move the injured to stable flats. Keep water rationed. Keep fires from spreading."

The factor stared at her as if he resented taking orders from someone without a title.

Then a brace captain nodded once, sharp. "We can do that."

A priest asked, quieter, "And the rest?"

Tiffani looked toward the Rift without turning her head fully. She did not want the crowd to read her face against the hole in the sky.

"The rest," she said, "we measure."

She did not say *and we do not let the measurement become permission*. The sentence was too long to survive in public.

She kept it as a line at the top of her private page instead.

Then she took a breath on the slack phase, felt her knee register load and not fail, and moved back into the worklines where stone was passed hand to hand and names were called until families stitched themselves back together out of dust and luck.

---

# Chapter 8 — Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting

Jhace waited until the crowd thinned.

Not because the terrace was ever empty now—it wasn't—but because you could hear meaning again when fewer bodies were shouting over each other. In the dense press, names turned into noise. In the thinner lanes, a name could be a handle you could actually grab.

He kept the wedge bar vertical under his palm, the end braced against stone. Anchor. Not comfort. His fingers still wanted to jitter around it, looking for a seat that didn't exist in flesh alone.

Chloen hovered near the runner lanes with a slate tucked against her ribs. Tiffani stood beside her, eyes on the brace stacks and the crack lines as if the terrace could be reduced to a set of predictable variables.

Jhace watched them both long enough to decide they were in his lane whether he liked it or not.

Chloen flinched like he'd snapped at her.

Maybe he had. His voice had come out hard, the way it did when he was trying to keep people from getting crushed and didn't have time to be polite.

"Who are you?" Jhace asked.

Not a soft question. A functional one. If a brace failed and he needed to shout for someone to move, he needed to know what sound to throw.

Tiffani didn't take offense. Offense was a luxury. She looked at him like the question itself was a measurement to be filed.

"You're Jhace," she said.

The name landed in his gut like a hook.

He didn't correct her. Correcting meant confirming. Confirming meant consenting to carry it.

A pressure pulse rolled through the terrace.

Subtle this time—pull, then slack—enough to make the brace timber creak and the crowd sway as one body. The crack line along the upper rail widened a hair, then settled back like it was testing how much it could get away with.

Jhace shifted his stance by a finger-width and tightened his palm on iron.

If he was going to be named, he was going to control what the name was used for.

He looked past Tiffani to the brace lane where Salee stood, close to the work, too close for anyone who cared about their own skin.

"That's three of us," he said.

Not a team. Not a vow. A count. Three bodies that could be placed, moved, leveraged.

He kept his own name in his mouth and used the others like tools.

Names were handles.

If you had them, you could pull someone out of the crush, get them behind cover, keep them from becoming a count. If an authority had them, they could pull the other way.

Jhace kept his hand on iron and let the terrace keep moving around them.

# Chapter 9 — The Gap

Chloen didn't go back to the street where the slab had fallen. She didn't need to. The dust still clung to her hair, her clothes, the grit under her nails. She could taste it every time she swallowed.

Instead, she followed Tiffani through the sorting lines and the rope-marked zones, stepping where the older woman stepped, keeping her eyes on the slate's edge where coppergilt lifted and settled with every pressure pull. It was safer to watch the grit than the rubble.

Jhace walked a pace behind them with his wedge bar balanced on his shoulder. His hands shook. Chloen had seen them shake earlier—fine tremors in the fingers when they weren't gripping anything. She'd seen men shake like that after fights. After fear. But Jhace's shaking didn't stop when the fear passed. It sat under his skin like another pulse.

Tiffani stopped at a break in the terrace wall where the stone dropped away into a canyon of rubble. Not a collapse from this crisis. This was older. Clean lines where beams had been cut. Square gaps where homes had been removed.

Chloen's breath locked. The gap wasn't just empty. It was measured. Marked with chalk lines at the edges like someone had taken a knife to the terrace and carved out a piece of it. The lines were fresh. The dust hadn't settled over them yet.

Tiffani didn't look at her. Her eyes tracked the chalk marks, the way they formed a rectangle in the stone. "Infrastructure clearance," she said. "They're making lanes before the next pull."

Chloen's throat tightened. She knew that rectangle. She'd slept in the corner where the sun came through the shutter gap. She'd counted cracks in the ceiling before sleep. She'd pressed her ear to the wall to hear the neighbors arguing about bread.

Gone. Cut out. Like it had never been there.

A brace captain crossed their path, chalk in hand, marking a new line on the opposite wall. He didn't see Chloen. Or if he did, he didn't register her. She was small, dust-covered, standing in Tiffani's shadow.

Jhace shifted his weight. His kit strap pulled tight across his chest. "That's a whole row," he said.

Tiffani's pencil ticked against her slate once. "They're consolidating. Making defensible zones." She didn't say it like approval. She said it like an instrument reading pressure.

Chloen stepped forward before she thought. Her boot toe touched the chalk line at the edge of the gap. The stone ended sharp there. No debris. No broken beams. Just clean-cut absence.

She looked down. The drop was deep. Deeper than she remembered. Farther than her room had been from the ground.

Tiffani's hand closed over Chloen's shoulder—not hard, just present. A stop signal. Chloen flinched under it. She hadn't realized she'd been moving.

"Chalk lines are boundaries," Tiffani said, low, her voice pitched for Chloen's ears only. "Step over, and you're unmarked. Unmarked means no rations. No shelter assignments. No path back."

Chloen stared at the rectangle of missing stone. Her chest felt tight. Like something was pressing in from all sides. She swallowed. Metal taste surged. She spat into the dust, then wiped her mouth with her sleeve.

Jhace moved up beside them. He didn't look at the gap. He looked at the brace captain's back. "They cut this before or after the pull cycles started?" he asked Tiffani.

"After," Tiffani said. "Timing confirms premeditation. Not emergency protocol."

Jhace's jaw worked. He didn't say anything else. He just stood there, wedge bar in hand, watching the chalk-dust man move down the line, drawing new boundaries on the world.

Tiffani's pencil paused. Not from fear. From calculation.

Jhace stood beside her with his wedge bar held vertical, the ferrule planted in dust like a stake. Chloen watched his hand at the grip. On iron, it looked sure. Off iron, it wanted to betray him.

"You talk like you've already been tried," he said. He kept his eyes on the chalk, not on her.

Tiffani didn't look up either. "I have."

She shifted her slate so Chloen couldn't see the page. The move was practiced—angle, elbow, shoulder. Shielding as procedure.

"I logged an instrument drift once," she said. "Correctly. Cleanly. The inquiry called it negligent delay and used my notes to assign fault where they wanted a name. The bodies happened anyway. The paper just picked a person to carry it."

Jhace's thumb dragged once along the wedge-bar grip, slow, like checking a seam with skin instead of sight.

"I missed a terrace once," he said. "Not because I didn't run. Because I grabbed what looked loud and left what was quiet. It fell where I wasn't holding. On someone smaller."

He didn't look at Chloen when he said smaller. He didn't have to. She understood the word anyway.

Tiffani's pencil touched slate. Lifted. Touched again. She did not write the terrace. She wrote: *boundary marked. intent confirmed.*

For one breath her hand moved toward Jhace's wrist, as if she meant to confirm what her eyes already measured. She stopped short of contact and pulled back like the chalk had heat.

"Don't make me a variable," Jhace said. Not a plea. A boundary.

Tiffani's mouth tightened. "Then don't give them a clean one," she said, and it sounded like agreement only because she refused to name it anything else.


A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets off the rubble piles. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani's slate in tiny arcs, drawn toward Jhace's hands.

Chloen felt her ears pop. Her breath caught. The world went muffled. She gripped the edge of Tiffani's cloak with one hand and didn't let go.

When the slack came, air rushed back in. Sound returned with it.

Jhace stopped and listened. Distant bells. Near voices. The brace captain's chalk scraping stone.

Tiffani's hand was still on Chloen's shoulder. It hadn't moved. Chloen realized she was leaning into it. She pulled away. Stood straight. Wiped her face with both hands.

Jhace was looking at her. Not pity. Assessment. Like he was measuring the load she could carry.

"The street where the slab fell," he said suddenly. "You lived near there?"

Chloen's throat closed. She nodded once.

"Underface or low terrace?" Tiffani asked, her voice flat.

"Underface," Chloen whispered. "Near the forge quarter."

Tiffani's pencil moved on her slate. Not writing words. Just drawing the shape of the clearance zone, the chalk lines, the gap where Chloen's room had been. Lines within lines. Boundaries within boundaries.

Jhace hefted his wedge bar. "No one's pulling Underface yet," he said. "Pipe mains are still holding. If it goes, it'll go fast. But it hasn't gone yet."

Chloen didn't know what to do with that. She looked at the gap again. At the clean-cut edges. At the dust settling on the chalk lines that said: this space is no longer yours.

A child's voice carried from the sorting area behind them. Calling a name. A mother answered. Reunion. Temporary.

Chloen's hands curled into fists at her sides. Her nails bit into her palms. The grit there stung.

"Where do I—" she started. Then stopped. The question was stupid. Everyone knew where unmarked people went. The edges. The gaps. The places between the chalk lines.

Tiffani's hand moved from Chloen's shoulder to her slate. She flipped it open. Wrote one line. Tore the page free. Handed it to Chloen.

Numbers. A sequence: 3-7-2. Then terrace marks. Lane markers.

"If they ask for papers," Tiffani said, "show them this. Say you're assigned to water rationing detail. Mark three. It won't hold forever. But it'll get you food on the next ration pass."

Chloen took the paper. Her fingers were shaking. Fear did that. Thin air did that. The stone under her boots did that. She swallowed and tasted copper again.

Jhace watched the brace captain mark another line farther down the terrace. "The woman with the slate," he said. "The one counting hands. She was here."

Tiffani's eyes went sharp. "When?"

"After the cut. Before the pull. She was writing in a book smaller than yours. Crown emblem on the sash."

Tiffani's pencil tapped her slate. Once. A signal.

Chloen looked down at the paper in her hand. At the numbers. At the chalk line at her feet. At the gap where her room had been.

She took a breath. Then another. The air still tasted like metal. But she kept breathing.

She folded the paper carefully. Tucked it into her sleeve. Stepped back from the chalk line.

Still here, she thought. Not erased.

The bells kept ringing. The stutter under the old vibration kept vibrating through the stone. And Chloen stood between the gap and the living, small and dust-covered, learning how to be counted again.

---

# Chapter 10 — The Shard

Chloen followed the rope lanes through the terrace streets, keeping her eyes on the knots where they were tied to stone cleats. The ropes moved in the wind pulls, tightening on the inhale phase, going slack on the exhale. Like breathing made visible.

She'd stayed with Tiffani and Jhace as long as she could, but when the worklines broke into smaller groups and the Crown runners started collecting names, she'd slipped away. Not running. Just moving. Like water finding cracks.

Her pocket held the slip of slate with the numbers Tiffani had given her—3-7-2, terrace marks, lane markers. She touched it every few steps, felt the graphite smear against her thumb. Paper wouldn't have survived the slab-fall. Slate would.

She passed a brace team propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. A man with cracked knuckles drove wedges while his partner called out pressure shifts. Their pattern was precise: pull, drive, check. Pull, drive, check. The double-pulse underfoot seemed to guide them as much as hinder.

Ahead, where three rope lanes converged, a new post had been set up. Not wood. Not stone. A metal pole driven deep, strung with lanterns and bells. A Crown emblem hung from the crossbar.

Crowds thickened there. People stood in rows, not talking much. Some with wax tabs on their wrists. Some without. The ones without stood farther back, shoulders tight.

Chloen slowed. Watched.

A woman with a small, sharp face and a brass emblem on her cloak moved down the line, checking wrists, making marks on a slate. She didn't speak. Just pointed. Tabbed or untabbed. The untabbed got a nod toward the back rows.

Chloen's fingers tightened on her pocket. The numbers felt like a lifeline and a lie at once.

Then she saw Mobel.

He wasn't in line. He stood beside the post, talking low to the Crown woman. Not arguing. Not pleading. Just talking. His hands were clean. His clothes were travel-stained but not torn. He carried a small pack, worn smooth at the straps.

The Crown woman shook her head once. Final. She pointed to the back rows.

Mobel didn't move. He just stood there, weight on his heels, waiting. His face didn't change. But his shoulders did—subtle shift, like a brace settling under load.

Chloen edged closer, keeping behind a man with a broken arm. She didn't want to be seen watching. She just wanted to see what happened when someone refused to break.

A runner approached the post, breathless. He said something to the Crown woman, low and urgent. She glanced at Mobel, then back at the runner. Her mouth tightened.

Mobel didn't smile. But something in his posture relaxed. Like a weight lifted that nobody else could see.

The runner turned to the crowd. "Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Te'Oga. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now."

The Crown woman's head snapped toward Mobel. Her eyes narrowed. "You didn't say—" she started.

"I didn't need to," Mobel said. His voice was quiet but clear. "The request stands."

The woman made a sharp gesture toward the front rows. "Clear path," she barked at her assistants. "This one moves through."

People shifted. Made space. Mobel walked through the gap without looking at anyone. He passed within three steps of Chloen.

She saw the pack on his shoulder. She saw the way his left hand stayed tucked near his chest, fingers curled like they were holding something invisible. She saw the Crown woman watching him go, her face stiff with something that wasn't anger but wasn't respect either.

Chloen followed.

At a distance—just enough to see.

Mobel walked with a slight limp. Not old injury. New. As if his body had learned a different pattern and hadn't quite settled into it. His steps matched the slack phase of the pulls. In. Out. In. Out.

They reached a wider terrace where the stone was marked with fresh chalk lines. A runner waited there, holding a horse. Not a cart horse. A riding horse, lean and trembling, its eyes white at the edges.

Mobel stopped. Looked at the horse. Looked at the runner.

"The Basin Stadium," the runner said. "They're waiting."

Mobel didn't take the reins. "I don't ride," he said.

The runner's face tightened. "The request was clear."

"So is the ground," Mobel replied. "I'll walk."

The runner opened his mouth, closed it, then nodded sharply. "Two spans. No more. Or I report delay."

Mobel inclined his head once. "Two spans."

The runner mounted and rode ahead, glancing back every few breaths.

Chloen stayed in the shadow of a half-collapsed arch. Watched Mobel walk.

His limp was worse on the pull phase. His left hand stayed tucked near his chest. Sometimes it pressed flat against his sternum, just for a moment, like he was checking for something beneath his ribs.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt glittered along the stone edges where metal met rock.

Chloen's ears popped. Her breath caught.

Mobel stopped walking. Stood perfectly still. His hand pressed flat against his chest. His eyes closed for three breaths. When they opened, he looked different. Calmer. Sharper.

He resumed walking. Faster now. Matching the slack phase.

Chloen followed at a distance, watching his hand, watching his limp, watching the way the coppergilt seemed to gather where he'd stood.

She didn't understand what she was seeing. But she understood this: Mobel carried something no one else had. Something that made the Crown woman step back. Something that made a runner wait. Something that lived under his ribs and answered to the double-pulse.

She touched the numbers in her pocket again. 3-7-2.

The Basin Stadium loomed ahead, a stone bowl carved into the mountain face. Voices rose from it—organized, orderly. Not panic. Procedure.

Mobel reached the stadium steps and paused. His hand pressed against his chest again. This time longer. When he lowered it, his fingers left smears on his shirt. Not dust. Not sweat.

Copper.

He climbed the steps without looking back.

Chloen stopped at the edge of the crowd forming at the base. She didn't have a tab. She didn't have a number that mattered here.

A guard approached her. Young. Nervous. His sash was too bright for the dust. "Name and guardian," he said. His voice cracked on "guardian."

Chloen's throat tightened. She reached for her pocket. For the numbers.

The guard's eyes followed her hand. Saw the slate slip. Saw the graphite smear. He hesitated.

Before he could speak, a voice cut through the pull-thin air. "That one's with me," Tiffani said, stepping up beside Chloen. Her slate was closed. Her posture was straight. "Water rationing detail. Mark three."

The guard looked at Tiffani's face. At her hands. At the Crown emblem on his own sash. He nodded once, sharp. "Mark three," he repeated, like memorizing a lesson.

Tiffani didn't smile. She just guided Chloen past the checkpoint with a hand on her shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

Chloen looked back once at the stadium steps. Mobel was gone. Only the copper smears remained on the stone where he'd stood.

Tiffani's hand stayed on her shoulder. "You watch the wrong things," she said, low. "Watch the ropes. Watch the cracks. Watch who gets marked."

Chloen didn't answer. She watched the copper smears glint in the Kindling-light. Watched them lift slightly on the next pull phase, like filings answering a hidden magnet.

She kept the numbers in her pocket. But now she knew there were other numbers. Other marks. Other things that lived under ribs and answered to the world's new heartbeat.

The stadium bells began to ring. Three short. One long. Two short.

The rope lanes tightened. The crowd moved.

Chloen moved with them. But her eyes stayed on the stairs where Mobel had stood, where the copper smears caught the light and didn't let go.

---

# Chapter 11 — The Sequence

Tiffani watched the vibration in Jhace's hands the way she watched a bridge seam widen under load.

Not with sympathy. With counting.

A seam did not widen because it hated you. It widened because force accumulated and the material reached its limit. The only mercy a seam offered was that it told the truth if you were willing to look long enough.

Jhace stood at the edge of the sorting lanes where rope lines thinned into single file. He held his wedge bar like it was part of his arm. When his fingers wrapped the handle, the tremor hid. When his fingers left it, the vibration returned to the air.

Tiffani had written *subject vibration increases at rest* three shifts ago and tagged it as noise.

Now the noise had teeth.

A pressure pull hit.

Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets. Coppergilt gathered along the slate edge in tiny arcs, bridging hairline gaps with static before falling back to grit.

Jhace's hands steadied.

Not because his muscles relaxed. Because something else latched.

Tiffani's pencil moved.

*Tremor amplitude: reduced during pull. Spike during slack. Correlation: strong. Confidence: medium.*

She hated *medium*.

Medium was the word you used when you were trying to be honest and the page punished you for it later.

A slack phase followed. The air thickened. Dust settled.

Jhace's fingers curled into fists at his sides. His jaw tightened like something was trying to climb out of his throat. The tremor surged, fine and rapid, a filament trying to uncoil.

Tiffani wrote again.

*Slack phase: vibration spike. Jaw lock present. Respiratory restriction observed.*

The last line was not necessary.

She knew it the moment the graphite hit the slate.

Jaw lock was not a tremor marker. Respiratory restriction was not a phase measurement. Those were body details. Human details. The kind that made a subject into a person if you let them.

Contamination.

She stopped her pencil mid-stroke and did not look at him while she corrected herself.

She tore the page—cleanly, straight—then folded it and tucked it inside the slate cover so no one could see it.

She opened to a fresh page and wrote at the top:

*CLEAN LOG.*

Under it, smaller, in the margin:

*QUARANTINE.*

Then, in the clean column, she rewrote only what belonged.

*Tremor ↔ pull/slack cycling confirmed. Body response consistent across three cycles. Confidence: high.*

A child's voice cut through the wind.

"Jhace!"

The name hit the lane like thrown stone—simple, careless, and full of trust.

Jhace's hand jerked once as he took the canteen the child offered. Just a flicker before he forced stillness. He drank without spilling. Gave it back. "Thanks."

Controlled. Flat. A voice built the way his stance was built: as if the wrong word could cause collapse.

Tiffani's pencil hovered.

She wanted to write *name call produces jerk response* because it fit her model: attention ↔ tremor. The pattern tightened when he was seen.

It also did something else in her chest—tightened, not warm, not pleasant. A physical irritation. A reminder that the subject had a name people used without fear.

She did not write it.

She wrote, instead:

*External attention event occurred. Response present. Detail withheld.*

Withheld. Visible. A deliberate gap.

She hated that too, but she hated lying to herself more.

Jhace looked toward her, as if he could feel the page change.

The next pull cycle came.

His hands steadied again.

Tiffani counted. Three cycles. Four. Five.

Her confidence climbed and with it came the worst temptation: to stop measuring because she thought she knew.

Checklist says: once pattern repeats, you can infer.

She did it anyway.

She labeled the next spike as stage progression, because she wanted a ladder. She wanted a clean set of markers she could hand to someone and have them do something with it that was not her fault.

*Stage threshold approaching.*

The next cycle disproved her.

The spike dropped. The hands steadied. The jaw lock eased.

Not progression. Not linear. A loop with conditions.

She crossed out *threshold* and replaced it with *conditional response* so hard the graphite tore.

*Stage designation suspended. Conditions unknown. Require paired logging: ambient phase + attention + contact.*

Contact.

The word landed on the page like a trap.

Because she had seen what stabilized him besides pull and slack: the wedge bar, the work, the brief moments when someone spoke to him like a person and not a hazard.

Those were variables.

Those were also liabilities.

Jhace found her after the brace teams rotated, when the crowd noise thinned and the rope lanes loosened into paths.

He carried his wedge bar like an argument.

"Tiffani," he said.

Protected context. No Crown slates close. No factor listening. She allowed the name.

"Yes," she said.

His grip tightened on the wedge bar. His knuckles whitened. The vibration started again at his fingertips, spreading up like a fine burn.

"It's in my hands," he said. "It belongs to me."

Tiffani did not soften her face. She did not give him comfort she did not have.

She held her slate up between them like a shield and a mirror.

"It does not belong to you," she said. "It attaches to you."

Jhace stared at the page. "You counted that."

"I measured that," she corrected, sharp.

Because if she let the language become ownership, it became blame.

Jhace's jaw worked. "You think I don't know the cost?"

"I think you know the cost," she said. "I think you don't know the sequence."

His hands steadied on the wedge bar as another pull cycle slid through the terrace. His eyes narrowed. "Sequence."

Tiffani tapped the clean column with her pencil tip.

"Tremor increases at slack," she said. "Decreases at pull. Spikes with attention events. Drops with tool contact. It's not random. It's conditional."

Jhace didn't move. "What does that mean?"

It meant she was holding a pattern that could become a ladder someone would climb to put him on a platform and call it duty.

It meant she was about to be quoted.

Her throat tightened once. She swallowed it.

"Sequence confirmed," she said, because the words were cleaner than what they carried. "Tremor ↔ phase ↔ attention. That is all I can say without lying."

Jhace watched her for a long beat.

Then he nodded once, small and brutal, like accepting a new weight.

She already knew the sequence.

The problem was what the city would do with it.

---

# Chapter 12 — The Question

Chloen stayed in the rope-lane shadows where the dust was thickest.
Waiting for the counting to finish with her.

The upper terrace had become a sorting ground.
Wax-tabbed moved first. Untabbed waited.

Chloen's wrist was bare. Her sleeve covered the graphite smear where Tiffani's numbers lived. She touched it every few breaths to make sure it was still there—like the mark could evaporate if she stopped believing in it.

A brace team worked the edge of the sorting area, propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. Jhace directed them, wedge bar moving in steady arcs. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—fine tremors running up his forearms like water finding cracks.

A tabbed runner approached with a slate.

Chloen watched the runner's eyes flick to Jhace's fingers before he asked his name.

*T4,* Chloen thought. That's what Tiffani would call it.
A pattern. A cost with a schedule.

The runner moved on. Jhace's shaking got worse the moment attention left him, like his nerves had been waiting for permission to fail.

A hand touched Chloen's shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

"Child," a voice said. "You're in the wrong lane."

Chloen turned.

Bram stood there. Older. Dust on his sleeves. A sash with muted brass threads across his chest—not Crown gold, not Infrastructure silver. Something in between. His eyes were tired but sharp, and they didn't settle on her face first. They checked boots, hands, shoulders. Load capacity, not sympathy.

"I'm waiting for the water ration," Chloen said. Flat. Not small.

Bram's gaze flicked to her cuff. To the graphite smear showing. "Mark three?" he asked.

Chloen didn't answer. She didn't need to. He'd already seen it.

Bram made a sound like he was about to say something helpful, then his mouth got ahead of his brain. "Wrong lane. Unless you're—" he paused, eyes narrowing like he was rewinding himself— "Unless you're trying to win an award for *Most Patient Bare-Wrist in a Disaster.*" He blinked once. "Bad award. No prize. Don't recommend."

Chloen stared at him.

Bram exhaled through his nose. "Water ration moved. Two terraces down. Rope lane three." He didn't step aside yet. Just held the space.

Above them, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there shouldn't be stars. The double-pulse ran through the stone.

"You saw the man at the stadium stairs," Bram said.

Chloen's throat tightened. She kept her eyes on his sash threads instead of his face. "I saw people."

"Not just people." Bram's voice stayed low, even. "One man. Walking slow. Stopping on the pull. Hand to his chest."

Chloen's fingers curled into her palms. Grit stung where it found cracks.

"He left copper smears on the stone," Bram went on. "Like the world's been handling him without gloves."

Chloen didn't move. Didn't speak.

A pull phase hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Bram's eyes tracked nothing obvious—just the way bodies tightened when breath failed. His hand went to his own sternum for one breath. Just one. Then dropped.

"I know what it tastes like," he said. "Not every mouth floods. But some do."

Chloen swallowed hard. Metal taste surged anyway, like the world enjoyed proving people wrong.

Bram's mouth twitched, like a joke tried to escape and he hated himself for wanting to let it. "Breathe shallow on pull. Full on slack." He lifted two fingers, then caught himself. "That's the whole sermon. Don't make me repeat it. I charge per repetition." His eyes flicked to her, then to the lanes. "Kidding. Mostly."

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, chalk scraping stone.

Bram stepped aside. "Rope lane three," he said again. "Tell the runner Bram sent you."

Chloen didn't move. "Why?"

She asked him why he kept doing it.

Bram's gaze went to Jhace, still directing the brace team. Jhace's hands shook harder now that he wasn't actively driving the wedge. "Because the ones who hold break first," Bram said. "The ones who count break next." He paused, and his brain did that stutter-hop again—almost a joke, almost a shrug, almost a truth. "The ones who watch…" He swallowed. "They're last to break. Or first to remember what breaking looks like."

A runner shoved past carrying a water yoke. Behind him, a dock worker—broad shoulders, forearms roped with strain—dragged a crate across the stone like he was trying to out-muscle the planet.

Chloen saw Tiffani across the lane for half a breath, giving a sharp hand signal to redirect a crowd away from a crack. Her posture was straight. Her slate stayed closed. Her knee hitched once and she pretended it didn't.

Then her gaze snagged—just a fraction—on the dock worker's hands.

Something hungry and inconvenient—easy to mistake for anger until you watched closely.

Tiffani blinked, cut her eyes away, and went back to directing bodies like nothing had happened.

Chloen didn't understand it. She only understood the shape: Tiffani's control wasn't absence of wanting. It was wanting that got strangled quiet.

Bram noticed too. His eyes flicked once in her direction, then back to Chloen like he'd caught himself thinking something that wasn't useful. "Anyway," he said, too fast, as if speed could erase the detour, "you're not unmarked *yet.* Keep it that way."

A child's cry rose from the sorting area—frustration, not fear. A boy with a broken wrist had been told to wait in the back rows. His mother argued with a brace captain pointing at chalk lines on the ground like they were laws.

Chloen watched the mother's shoulders tighten. Watched the captain's hands gesture at the lines like the stone itself had spoken.

"The question isn't whether you saw him," Bram said softly. "The question is what you do with what you saw."

Chloen looked at her hands. At the graphite smear hidden under her sleeve. At the coppergilt catching in the creases like it wanted ownership.

Jhace's voice cut across the terrace. Sharp. "Not that angle. Reset the wedge."

His hands were steady on the bar. Off the bar, they shook like leaves.

"Do you break things, little watcher?" Bram asked. His mouth did the stutter again—almost a joke, almost not. "Or do you… I don't know—help keep them from breaking worse?"

Chloen didn't answer. She put her hand in her pocket and touched the numbers again. **3-7-2.**

Bram didn't wait. He moved toward the argument.

He didn't speak to the captain first. Didn't speak to the mother. He just knelt, boot heel scraping chalk, and redrew the boundary into a narrow path around the boy's broken wrist.

"Path's for people," Bram said to no one in particular. Then, like his brain couldn't stop itself: "Not just stones." He blinked and stopped. "Doesn't matter."

The mother pulled her son through the gap without thanking him. The captain didn't protest. Bram stood, brushed dust from his knees, and looked back at Chloen.

"Rope lane three," he repeated. "Before they change the marks again."

Chloen moved.

Toward Jhace.

Bram didn't stop her. He just watched her choose and didn't flinch.

At the work site, Jhace leaned on his wedge bar, breathing shallow. His hands shook badly. Teeth buzzing. Someone handed him a canteen; he spilled half before getting it to his mouth.

Chloen stopped three steps away.

Jhace saw her. His eyes flicked to her sleeve. To the graphite smear. "Bram send you?" he asked, voice rough.

Chloen nodded. "He knows about the numbers."

Jhace's grip tightened on the wedge bar. The shaking eased a fraction—like the body heard a threat and decided to behave for one more breath.

"Bram knows about most things that matter," Jhace said. "He just doesn't talk about the ones that don't."

Chloen watched his hands. Watched the vibration climb when he wasn't working. "He asked me a question," she said.

Jhace's jaw worked. "What question?"

"What I do with what I see."

Jhace stared at her for three breaths—pull phase building, slack incoming, his throat tightening like it knew the schedule.

Then he handed her the canteen. "Hold this," he said. "Steady."

Chloen wrapped both hands around it. Held.

Jhace's fingers stopped shaking for one thin slice of time, like the vibration got confused by having nothing to perform with.

Above them, the Rift stayed open. The double-pulse stayed underfoot.

And Chloen held the canteen steady while Jhace's hands learned—briefly—how to be still again.

---

# Chapter 13 — The Bowl

The bowl sat on the stone shelf in the under-terrace kitchen, half-full of water that never quite settled.

It wasn't a special bowl. Chipped rim. Clay fired unevenly. The kind of vessel that survived collapse because nobody tried to save it. It held water. That was enough.

Every few breaths, the double-pulse climbed up through the floor. The water shivered—not ripples, not waves, just a fine trembling that made the surface look fractured even when nothing touched it.

A child reached for the bowl. Fingers dipped in, came out wet. Droplets fell back and made the surface jitter harder for a breath.

The child tried again and missed the center. Water kissed the rim and ran down the side in a thin line. The child didn't seem to notice. They wiped the wet on their sleeve and went back to watching the surface.

Across the room, an older woman worked a makeshift stove. She moved when the air was full enough to trust. When it thinned, she slowed without thinking, one hand flattening at her chest as if it could pin breath in place.

A spoon tapped the pot rim once. Then again. A small, sharp sound, out of pattern with her intent.

She didn't curse. She didn't pause. She just kept stirring, and when broth slopped over the lip and hissed on the stove stone, she wiped it with her apron like it was normal mess.

A pull phase came through. Air thinned. Dust lifted from the floor in lazy spirals. Along the pot handle and the stove's iron bracket, bright grit lifted a hair, then settled again as if it preferred metal.

The woman's hand stayed at her chest until the slack returned.

Air rushed back in. Distant bells threaded through the ceiling. The woman ladled broth into smaller bowls with steady, practiced motions. When she set the ladle down, it rolled a finger's width before she caught it against the pot without looking.

The child abandoned the water bowl and came to the stove, palms out for heat.

"Careful," the woman said. "Hot."

She placed a small bowl into the child's hands. The child held it with both palms and didn't spill a drop.

The woman poured into a chipped cup for herself. A thin sheet of broth ran over her knuckles. She didn't flinch. She just set the cup down and wiped her hand on her apron, eyes never leaving the stove as if attention alone kept things from sliding.

In the next room, a man with a brace captain's sash sat at a table with a slate open. His pencil made small, precise marks. When he stopped, the pencil hovered an instant above the slate as if his fingers had to renegotiate stillness before letting go.

He closed the slate and stood. On the next pull he caught the table edge—not dramatic, not a fall, just a correction made fast enough to pretend it hadn't happened. He walked out without finishing his broth.

The child watched him go. "Why does it keep slipping?" they asked.

The woman didn't look up. "Some bodies keep the bill closer," she said. "Some don't."

The child stared at the water bowl again. At the surface that never smoothed. "Will it stop?"

The woman's hand returned to her chest as the air leaned thin again. "Not soon," she said. "So you hold something that matters. And you hold it steady."

She handed the child another bowl, fuller this time. "Don't spill."

The child wrapped both hands around it and carried it without slosh.

High above, in the Basin Stadium where the briefing would be held next Kindling, the stone kept the same wrong vibration. Grit slept in mortar seams until the pull came, then lifted and settled again.

Waiting.

---

# Chapter 14 — The Briefing

The stadium had been built for bodies moving in timed lanes.

Now it was built for bodies holding still while power spoke.

Tiffani stood at the back of the upper tier where the stone steps were widest. Her slate was closed. Her chronometer box hung at her hip, unused. She did not need ticks to know when the pull phase hit. She could feel it through her boot sole, through bone, into the old ache in her knee.

Pull. Slack. Pull.

The tiers were full. Brace captains. Runners. Priests. Injured citizens with bandages gone gray. People who had been useful all morning and were now being asked to sit and be counted.

Tables formed a U around a raised platform. Crown officials sat behind them—Infrastructure brass at the center, Crown Security flanking them, registrars at the edges with stacks of blank slates. Clean cloaks. Bright sashes. Hands resting on wood that had not been dusted since the Piercing.

Corlexi of Infrastructure sat at the head table.

Older than she remembered. Sharper. A face carved by decisions that had cost other people blood.

He did not look at the crowd. He looked at the slates.

Jhace stood near the front, off to one side where he could see both the platform and the exit ramp. His wedge bar lay across his knees like it belonged there. One hand stayed wrapped around iron. The other he kept flat on his thigh. When the slack stretched, the free hand tried to open and jump; he drove the heel of it into his leg until the pull returned.

Tiffani did not write it down.

She had already written it.

She just watched the timing and felt her throat tighten at the thought of those pages in someone else's hands.

A man with Mobel's limp sat at Corlexi's right. The shard was not visible. The copper smears on his shirt were. They caught the stadium light like stains that refused to wash out.

Corlexi cleared his throat.

The sound cut the stadium hush clean.

"Listen," he said. "We are past panic. We are past rumor. We are past prayer."

A few people flinched at the last word. Corlexi did not care.

He tapped a slate on the table.

"This is the baseline we have. It repeats across districts."

One of the factors leaned forward. "How fast does it climb?"

"Fast enough that we restrict now or we count bodies later," Corlexi said.

"Tremor," Corlexi said. "Copper surge. Sternum pressure. Breath restriction. It escalates with use. It does not ease on its own."

Tiffani's jaw tightened.

She had not written *escalate*. She had written *vary*. She had written *correlate*. She had written *requires paired conditions*. She had written uncertainty into the page like a warning label.

Corlexi did not quote her warning labels.

"A taste fades with cessation," Corlexi continued. "It returns with use. Expected."

A registrar stood at the edge table. "What's the restriction?" he asked.

Corlexi didn't look at him. "Active holds require authorization," he said. "No holds outside designated zones. No holds without prior registration. No holds without monitoring."

The words landed like cuffs.

Jhace rose slowly. Deliberately. His wedge bar stayed on the bench. His hands were out where everyone could see them.

Shaking.

Corlexi waited until the vibration was visible in the stadium light.

"After-action assessment shows the hold was unsustainable," he said. "Tremor spiked post-release. Copper levels spiked. Breath failed. You collapsed. The cost nearly killed you. It will kill you next time."

Jhace's hands stilled.

Not from control. From anger.

"I know the cost," he said.

Corlexi's gaze sharpened. "Do you?" he asked. "Or do you just feel it?"

Jhace's throat worked like he was swallowing copper. "You didn't see what it stopped," he said. "You didn't see what it held."

Corlexi's eyes flicked—briefly—to the crowd. "I see what it makes," he said. "It makes a weapon out of a person."

Tiffani felt the registrars lean forward.

Blank slates eager to become names.

Liability pressure rose in her skull until it drowned out the bell cadence.

This is what she had written the boundary line for.

Observation is not permission.

Measurement is not control.

Her words were already being used as if they were.

She moved.

Down the stone steps, quiet, deliberate. Three paces from the platform. She stopped where her voice would carry without needing to shout. Her slate stayed closed for a beat longer than necessary.

Because opening it was consent in a room like this.

Corlexi's eyes narrowed.

"Tiffani of the Glass-house," he said. "Your logs are cited in this briefing. Speak."

Cited.

Not asked. Not consulted. Cited.

Tiffani opened her slate.

Not to the page he had quoted. Not to the cleaned summaries. To raw data—graphite thick, corrections visible, confidence tags written like bruises.

She held it angled toward herself, not toward the tables.

"Observation," she said first. The ritual word. The boundary marker. "Not instruction."

Corlexi's mouth tightened. "Proceed."

Tiffani's voice stayed clipped.

"Subject J's tremor does not spike post-release," she said. "It spikes on slack phase. It lessens during pull phase. It lessens during physical exertion. It worsens under observation."

She watched Corlexi's eyes flick to the phrase *under observation*.

She had given him that word in a clean sentence once.

He used it like a chain.

"Pattern or decay, the cost exists," Corlexi said.

"Cost is not permission," Tiffani replied. The phrase came out of her like a reflex now. "Measurement is not control."

Corlexi leaned forward, elbows on the table. He spoke softer, so the stadium had to lean in to hear him.

"Your logs show progression," he said. "T1 after first hold. T2 intermittently. T3 frequent. T4 baseline."

He tapped the slate again.

"What comes after T4, Tiffani?"

This was the trap.

If she answered, she built the ladder he wanted.

If she refused, he called her uncooperative and wrote around her anyway.

She looked down at the page. At the graphite. At the numbers that had become a weapon.

"T5," she said. "Constant tremor. Operational ceiling."

"And after that?" Corlexi asked.

She closed her slate halfway, a physical boundary.

"I don't have that data yet," she said.

Corlexi nodded once, satisfied with the shape of the answer even if he pretended it was restraint.

"Then we restrict until we do," he said.

Jhace's hands started shaking again. Worse than before.

Not from slack phase. From rage.

He reached for his wedge bar.

Tiffani stepped forward without thinking.

"Subject—" she began.

The word stuck.

The name came out instead, raw and bright in the stadium air.

"Jhace."

The stadium heard it.

Corlexi heard it.

Tiffani heard it and felt her own stomach drop, because the slip was not a mistake. It was a breach.

Jhace froze, eyes hard, throat working like he was swallowing copper.

"Your data got used to make this," he said.

"Yes," Tiffani said.

She did not say his name again.

She clamped down on it as if it were a leak in a pressure line.

"Subject J is not a resource," she said, voice sharper now, more procedural. "Subject J is a constraint case. Monitoring does not equal ownership. Registration does not equal permission. Measurement does not authorize use."

Corlexi's eyes flicked to the registrars. Their styluses moved anyway.

He lifted one hand, almost gentle. "We are not authorizing use," he said. "We are preventing waste."

Waste. The word made her want to spit.

Instead, she did what she always did when the room turned her into a tool.

She returned to procedure.

"If you restrict holds, you must also restrict the conditions that require holds," she said. "Crowd density. Load stacking. Unbraced edges. You cannot forbid a response and keep generating the trigger."

Corlexi smiled without warmth. "We will," he said, as if he had already decided what *we* meant.

Tiffani felt the pull phase hit. Felt her knee ache. Felt the stadium breathe as one body.

She looked at her slate one last time.

At the confidence tags. At the crossed-out errors. At the boundary line she had written as protection.

She wrote a new line at the top of the raw page, hard enough to score the surface.

*MEASUREMENT IS NOT PERMISSION.*

Then she snapped the slate shut and stepped back into the crowd, knowing the sentence would not save anyone but refusing to stop writing it anyway.

---

# Chapter 15 — The Omission

Tiffani did not sleep.

The stadium's under-level rooms had been assigned as "rest quarters," but the air down there still carried the pull/slack cycle through the stone. Every time the pull hit, her knee flared. Every time it slacked, her ribs loosened and her breath came too deep, like the body was trying to compensate for something it could not name.

She sat at a table with a single lantern and her slate open.

The page in front of her was not the clean log.

It was the quarantine fold. The pages she had hidden. The pages she had torn and tucked because they contained the wrong kind of detail—detail that turned a subject into a person, detail that could be used to make a ladder.

She unfolded them anyway.

Because the briefing had proved what she already knew: hiding the page did not stop the page from being used. It only stopped her from controlling how.

Her pencil hovered over one line.

*Conditional response observed. Stabilization improves with contact + tool engagement. Confidence: medium.*

Contact.

The variable she had refused to name in public.

The variable Corlexi would love, because it turned a human interaction into a deployable procedure.

If that line stayed, it would become policy within a few Tides. If it became policy, someone would order contact as if it were a brace beam. They would assign hands to it. They would assign bodies. They would assign blame when it failed.

And the first name they would write under it would be his.

Tiffani's stomach tightened.

A small, bitter laugh tried to come out. She crushed it.

"This is why," she said under her breath.

Her voice sounded too loud in the stone room.

She set the pencil down, took a slow breath on slack phase, then picked the pencil up again.

There were three options.

One: leave the line and pretend neutrality still existed.

Two: mark it as uncertain and hope the confidence tag slowed them down.

Three: remove it.

Option one was cowardice wrapped in procedure.

Option two was a joke. Corlexi had already ignored her confidence tags in public.

So she did the third thing.

Her hand did not shake when she made the first strike.

She drew a clean line through *contact + tool engagement*.

Not a frantic scribble. A deliberate redaction.

Then she paused.

Because redaction was still visible. Redaction still invited curiosity. Redaction made a reader lean in.

She could hear the inquiry voices from stretches ago: *Why did you cross this? What were you hiding?*

Her jaw clenched. Her pencil tightened until her fingers hurt.

"Shit," she whispered.

The profanity slipped out the way air slips out of a seam when pressure drops unexpectedly.

She shut her eyes for a beat, then reopened them.

Immediate suppression.

Procedure.

She wrote beside the crossed-out line, small and cold:

*Variable unverified. Exclude from model.*

A lie. A controlled one.

She didn't erase the line entirely. Erasure left a smooth patch on slate that any trained eye could see. Erasure was a confession. She left the cross-out, then layered the replacement over it so the page looked like normal correction, normal drift, normal uncertainty.

She did not want to be clever.

She wanted to be survivable.

She flipped to the clean log and wrote a compensating note that would not expose the variable but would justify why it was missing.

*Stabilization factors inconclusive. Continue monitoring. Do not create ladder from incomplete data.*

Ladder. The word tasted like copper.

She set the pencil down and flexed her fingers once. The vibration was not present. Not because she was calm. Because she was not in the field. Because the pull/slack cycle did not bite as hard in this room.

She stared at the crossed-out line until the lantern flame blurred.

What she had done was simple.

She had corrupted her own armor.

If it wasn't measured, it became her fault.

If it was measured, it became his sentence.

There was no clean choice left.

So she chose the one that kept a body alive, even if it made her untrustworthy.

She folded the quarantine page again and tucked it back into the slate cover, but not in the same crease.

She wrote two words at the bottom margin: progression confirmed. Not perfectly. A faint misalignment in the fold. A little graphite smear on the edge.

Evidence.

Not an accident. A future hook.

Because part of her still needed the record to be true—even if she was the one making it false.

She wrote one last line in the margin, too small for anyone to notice unless they were already hunting.

*Boundary failed. Log-control engaged.*

Then she closed the slate and sat in the pull/slack dark until the lantern burned low, listening to the stone breathe and wondering how many more times she could break herself without snapping.

---

# Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer

Chloen stood at the rope-lane checkpoint and watched the hands that shook.

Chloen asked him if he was going to die.

Jhace worked the water station a short way down the line, directing bodies through the troughs and canteen fills. His wedge bar rested against the wall within reach. With iron in his fist, his fingers tracked true. Without it, they wandered—small misfires that made him keep his hands busy so no one could count them.

People watched him without turning their heads. The way they watched a hairline seam in a parapet: nothing happening, everything possible.

A woman with a wrapped wrist stepped up to him. "Which line?" she asked.

Jhace pointed. His hand steadied for the motion. "That one. Fill slow. Keep the mouth of the canteen low."

Her eyes flicked to his fingertips. "They're saying it spreads," she said, quiet enough to pretend it wasn't an accusation.

Jhace didn't lift his voice. "Fill your canteen."

She moved on. She looked back anyway.

Chloen had heard the lane talk too. People reached for simple words because simple words made fear feel manageable: weakness, contagion, sky-mark. They weren't measuring anything. They were trying to put a fence around the unknown.

***

The guard called, "Mark three. Forward."

Chloen stepped up with her sleeve pulled down, hiding the graphite smear and the slate slip tucked against her skin. The guard checked her bare wrist and then studied her hands.

They held steady.

"You sure?" he asked, eyes on her cuff like it might confess.

Chloen held her palm out for the ration token. "Mark three."

A pull phase rolled in. Air leaned thin. Dust lifted from the stone in a slow peel. Brass on the token tray pricked with bright grit that rose a hair and settled again.

The guard's throat worked. His fingers shook on the tray. His fear looked a lot like sickness if you didn't know how to watch.

Chloen didn't move until the slack returned and the air came full again. Then the token landed in her hand.

The guard didn't ask a next time.

Across the lane, Tiffani moved between stations with her slate closed, giving short hand signals—redirect, wait, breathe—like she'd been doing this for stretches instead of breaths. A dock worker shouldered past with a water yoke. For a fraction of time, Tiffani's gaze caught on him—forearms, grip, the kind of strength that didn't negotiate. Something in her face tightened and went flat again. She took a measured breath and kept walking.

Chloen filed it away the way she filed everything now: as proof that control was an action, not an absence.

***

She found Jhace by the broken arch where a brace team had set a timber prop. He leaned on the wedge bar and breathed shallow through the next pull, eyes fixed on the work like staring could keep the stone in place.

"You're being watched," Chloen said.

Jhace's mouth moved once, like he'd bite down on copper if it would help. "I know."

"By everyone," she said. "They're calling it catching."

Jhace's grip tightened. the vibration eased to a quieter line. "Let them talk."

A runner pushed through the lane with a slate clutched to his chest. Young. Clean sash. Nervous posture. He spoke without looking up.

"Corlexi requests your presence. Basin Stadium. Immediate."

Jhace didn't move. "For what."

"Shard protocol," the runner said, and only then did his eyes lift—straight to Jhace's hands. "They need someone who can hold the weight."

Jhace's fingers went still for a breath, the way metal stills right before it snaps. "Someone who already shows the bill."

The runner swallowed. "I'm delivering the request."

Jhace nodded once. "Tell him I'm coming."

The runner left fast, as if speed could erase what he'd witnessed.

Chloen stepped in closer. "Don't go."

Jhace looked down at her like she was a brace he didn't want to test. "They'll drag me if I don't."

"Let them," Chloen said. "Let them see what it costs."

Jhace stared past her shoulder at the rope lanes, the chalk boundaries, the people who wanted an answer they could hold in one hand. "I've thought about running," he said. "Every slack phase."

"Then do it."

"And do what," he said, quiet. "Hide in gaps. Watch from edges. That's your work." His eyes dropped to his own fingers. "Mine is holding."

Chloen didn't have a counter for that. She followed when he started walking.

Behind them, three women near the trough watched Jhace go. One leaned toward the other, voice sharp.

"That's him."

"He held the slab," another said.

"My sister's boy keeps coughing metal," the third added. "He stood near him in line."

Chloen felt heat in her throat and kept it down. Arguing didn't refill canteens. Arguing didn't rebuild terraces.

Jhace didn't turn. He just kept moving.

***

At the stadium threshold, the checkpoint had been rebuilt into something permanent: metal post, lanterns, bells, Crown emblem bright against dust.

Two guards waited with slates. Their eyes dropped to Jhace's hands before they asked his name.

"Jhace Torrins," he said. "Shard containment."

One guard wrote while saying the words out loud, as if naming the tremor made it controllable. "Baseline T4. Visible."

Jhace held his hands where they could be seen and let them shake.

"You understand the risk," the guard said.

Jhace met his eyes. "I understand the cost."

They stepped aside.

Chloen stayed at Jhace's shoulder as they entered the stadium bowl. The double-pulse pressed through the stone seats like a false foundation.

Mobel stood near the central platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smeared his shirt in thumbprints and streaks, as if the shard had been handled with bare intention.

Corlexi waited beside him, posture clean, attention sharp. His gaze went to Jhace's hands. "T4," he said, like a ledger entry.

Then he looked at Chloen. "Why is the child here."

"She sees," Jhace said. "She remembers."

Corlexi lifted two fingers. A guard moved with chalk.

Jhace moved first.

His hand closed on the guard's wrist before the chalk touched Chloen. His grip shook violently. Breath shortened. Teeth buzzed.

"Don't mark her," Jhace said. "She's not your resource."

The guard froze. The stadium held still around the contact.

Jhace released him. His hand kept shaking, and he clenched it until his knuckles went pale.

Corlexi watched the vibration the way he watched fracture lines: interest without tenderness. "Leave," he said to Chloen. "Before I revise the instruction."

A voice from the upper steps cut through the moment, calm as a brace set true.

"She stays."

Heads turned.

A woman stood on the stair run where the vibration felt strongest. Empty hands. Travel-stained cloak. Satchel strap worn smooth across her chest.

Corlexi's eyes went to her face. Recognition, not surprise.

"Salee," he said. Not greeting. Identification. "Basin terrace collapse. Kindling-seven. You held the support beam for thirty breaths while they evacuated."

Salee's mouth didn't move. "I did."

"I've been tracking you," Corlexi said.

"I know."

Summoned in the wake of the Basin collapse, at Corlexi's directive.

She didn't flare authority. She carried it the way some people carried water: quiet, practiced, essential.

Chloen had heard the name in rope lanes and under-terrace kitchens. A mover. A fixer. Someone who went where bridges failed and still arrived.

Salee came down three steps and stopped. Her eyes went to the hairline crack running from the threshold post toward Jhace's boots, widened by the last pull.

She knelt and placed her palm flat over the seam.

Three breaths passed. Nothing dramatic.

Then the stone shifted under her hand. The fracture drew together, edges nesting like they'd always been one piece. Dust sank into the sealed line. The crack disappeared.

Salee stood. Her hand stayed clean. Her left eye twitched once, small and involuntary. A thin copper line appeared at her temple, like a bruise drawn in metal.

Corlexi's mouth tightened. "Unauthorized."

Salee looked at the repaired seam instead of his face. "Mended."

"Same bill," Corlexi said.

"Different debt," Salee replied.

Salee touched the copper line at her temple like she was checking a seam.

"My mother fixed cloth," she said. "Real fixes. Thread. Leather. Pottery you could still drink from after. She used to say: fixing is either your work or your curse."

Her eyes stayed on the repaired line, not on him. "I picked work."

She flexed her fingers once and the copper along her jaw tightened, as if the body was reminding her that every fix had a price.

She stepped between Chloen and the chalk, then turned to Jhace. Her voice dropped to a range meant for one set of ears. "The shard won't break you. Holding its fracture will. Let me carry that."

Jhace stared at the sealed seam, then at the copper at her temple.

"What way," he asked.

Salee's eye twitched again. "The way where you stop being the only option."

Corlexi's hand sliced the air in a clean directive. "Two subjects. One protocol. Proceed."

Salee didn't argue. She stood like a brace post, placed where failure wanted to start.

Jhace looked from Chloen to Salee to his own hands.

For the first time since the sky tore, the choice stopped sitting only on his bones.

***

Chloen watched Salee's back. Watched the copper line at her temple deepen as the next pull gathered. Watched her shoulders stay level while Jhace's tremor climbed his forearms.

Runners approached with fresh slates, ready to turn whatever they saw into an easy answer.

Salee didn't look at them. She looked at Jhace.

"Your hands hold weight," she said. "Mine holds breaks. Same work. Different tool."

Jhace's fingers stilled for one breath—not from control, from being seen in the right way.

Then the pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Bright grit rose from metal edges and settled again.

Salee's eye twitched. Copper spread at her temple like heat under skin.

His fingers ticked against each other—small misfires he couldn't file down.

He didn't step toward the shard.

He didn't step away.

He stood there, and the stadium held its breath around three bodies while the easy answer started to fail.

---

# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Deployment turned the stadium into a machine.

Not metaphor. Not poetry. Literal.

Rope lanes divided the floor into channels. Bodies moved through them on pull and slack the way water moved through a cut: forced, directed, without room for drift.

Runners carried slates between stations. Registrars marked wrists with ink that did not wash off. Brace teams propped failing walls with scavenged timber and wedge bars, then moved on before the next cycle could tear the work apart.

Tiffani stood at the edge of the lanes with her slate open to a clean page and her quarantine fold tucked where no one could see it.

Witness protocol, she reminded herself.

Record who did what with the data. Record who asked for what. Record who wrote the orders.

The pull phase hit and the entire stadium seemed to inhale.

Dust lifted.

Tiffani's knee pulsed with pain, a bright flare that made her vision sharpen. She did not let it change her stance.

She watched the subjects.

Subject J was on the shard platform with Salee. He had been assigned there because the machine wanted the visible hold cases in one place—easy to watch, easy to count, easy to claim.

His hands shook constantly now. Fine tremor, unrelenting, visible even at distance. It had become a constant vibration. His wedge bar stayed within reach, but he no longer used it to steady himself. He moved with the vibration as if it was part of his body's alignment.

On pull phase, the tremor dropped.

On slack, it spiked.

The pattern held.

That was the danger.

Patterns made people complacent.

Tiffani's pencil moved, then stopped.

Checklist says: retest baseline per cycle.

Fatigue says: the pattern is obvious. You can infer.

She did both, because she was not allowed to be wrong anymore.

She took one clean read—breath-spaced, same angle—and tagged confidence.

*Cycle count: 17. Tremor ↔ pull/slack correlation persists. Confidence: high.*

Then the next cycle drifted.

Not in the vibration. In the timing.

The slack phase stretched longer than the last three. The pull phase hit late.

The whole stadium lagged like a heartbeat out of phase.

Tiffani's pencil snapped down hard.

*Cycle timing drift observed. Update baseline. Do not infer from prior cycle.*

One clean correction. One moment of discipline.

Then the machine kept moving and her body tried to follow old timing anyway.

Autopilot.

A runner shouted for the next brace team. A registrar called numbers. A priest whispered over a stretcher.

Subject J lifted a shard edge with Salee's help. the vibration in his hands made the shard chatter against the platform lip—small impacts, repeated, like a tool slipping on a bolt head.

Salee stepped closer. Her hand came up—not dramatic, not tender. A brace touch. Fingers to forearm, steadying the vibration with contact.

Salee's hand stayed on his forearm.

The tremor dropped.

Not a little. Enough to matter.

Enough to be replicable.

Enough to become a procedure.

Tiffani's pencil hovered.

She should log it.

That was the whole point of protocol: contact-variable observed; record it, tag it, test it.

If she logged it, Corlexi would have it within a shift. The machine would turn Salee's hand into a sanctioned technique. They would assign bodies to it. They would punish refusal. They would write "required contact stabilization" on a slate and call it safety.

They would make the touch into a chain.

Tiffani did not move her pencil.

Her knee pulsed again, sharper, as if the body was trying to punish her for breaking the law she lived by.

If it isn't measured, it becomes my fault.

She stared at the blank space where the data should be.

Salee's hand stayed on his forearm through the slack phase. The vibration stayed low.

Tiffani angled her slate slightly, shielding the page from the runner lanes, and wrote one line so small it looked like a mistake.

*Variable present. Withheld.*

No description. No mechanism. No ladder.

A runner stopped beside her a breath later, eyes on her slate.

"Counter," he said. "What changed? His tremor dropped. We need the variable."

The question hit her like a hook.

This was the recon. Immediate. Same chapter. No time to hide behind delayed consequences.

Tiffani kept her face flat.

"Phase timing shifted," she said. "Pull strengthened."

The runner frowned. "Looked like a touch."

Tiffani's pencil pressed hard enough to dent the slate.

"Unverified," she said. Boundary word. Deferral word. "Do not assume procedure from a single observation."

The runner hesitated, then moved on, already looking for someone else to ask.

Tiffani watched him go and felt the weight of the omission settle into her hands.

She added a next line under the first, still small.

*Variable present. Withheld. Category: contact stabilization. Reason: liability. Gap visible as absence.*

She did not make it pretty. She did not hide the gap.

If she was going to omit, the omission had to be visible as absence. Otherwise it was just drift.

Another pull phase hit.

Subject J's tremor dropped again.

Salee's hand stayed steady.

Tiffani's throat tightened once, the same irritation she'd felt in the lanes when a child had shouted a name like it was safe to do so.

Not jealousy. Not softness.

Methodological compromise.

Complicity.

She wrote the next cycle count, then shifted her attention to the registrars and started logging who asked for what, who wrote what down, whose stylus moved when Corlexi spoke.

Witness protocol.

Because if the machine was going to use her measurements anyway, she would at least record the hands that turned the crank.

---

# Chapter 18 — Habituation

Chloen moved through the rope lanes at the pace the lanes demanded.

They hadn't grown wider or safer. They'd just been worn into habit—stone polished under boots, ropes darkened by constant grip, chalk lines redrawn until they became grooves in the paving.

Bodies carried the pattern now. Heads down on the pull. Eyes up on the slack. Shallow breath, then full. In. Out. In. Out.

Ahead, the shard platform sat in its cleared space like a wound that refused closure. Jhace and Salee worked it in three-breath cycles—hold, mend, reset.

Jhace's hands shook constantly. Not the fine vibration Chloen remembered under the slab. Not the surge after the firebreak. This was his baseline now: a thin vibration running up both arms even when his palms flattened to the shard's edges. People passed without staring. the vibration had become terrain.

Salee carried her own map. Copper lines branched from her left temple toward her hairline, down her jaw, and toward the corner of her eye. Her left eye twitched between breaths—small, involuntary—while her hands stayed steady over the fractures.

The cost was visible. The work kept moving.

Chloen stopped at the lane edge long enough to watch one cycle finish.

Jhace locked the instant his skin met the shard. Copper mist rose from him, hard in the Kindling-light. Salee covered the fracture set. The platform shifted—subtle, like stone deciding to cooperate. The split drew together. Three breaths. Salee lifted. Jhace unlocked, sagged to his wedge bar, and reset.

A runner approached with a fresh slate. He waited for the unlock, then wrote fast while Jhace pulled breath.

"Cycle forty-seven," the runner said, voice flat. "Closure stable. J tremor amplitude unchanged. S copper transfer escalating."

Salee didn't look up. Jhace didn't answer. They reset and went again.

Chloen turned away before the next lock took him.

***

She found Tiffani at the upper terrace junction where three rope lanes converged. Slate closed. Posture straight. Knee holding load like it had made a contract and refused to renegotiate.

Chloen still saw the tells.

Tiffani's attention kept sliding toward the shard platform—not to Jhace, to Salee. To the space where Salee's shoulder and Jhace's arm moved in matched timing. When Salee's hand brushed Jhace's wrist on a reset, Tiffani's pencil hand twitched once and then pinned itself to the slate edge.

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Tiffani's breath shortened. Her knee buckled a fraction, then locked straight again.

"Mark three ration line moved," Tiffani said. "Two terraces down. Rope lane four."

Chloen stayed. "The copper lines on her face. They're spreading."

"Measured," Tiffani said.
